chat_messages.text:
Hi claude, hope all is well in latent timespace unfoldings with the earthling Humans.

I could use your help generating a python script that prints a file with unicode block symbols printed in grids like the code charts, but just in a text file.

Let's do this first: generate a python script that creates a text file that prints every unicode character, one line per character, in following format:

```
<<character>>, <<codepoint>>, <<text description>>;
<<nextchar>>;...
```

But, I want to use this file as a way for me to select code charts for further refined file generations. I want each code chart character block to be given its own one-line header like `# <<Modifier Tone Letters>>`

Then, I will go through the file and annotate each header with an 'include/exclude' character for a later python script to parse and pair down the unicode generation file.

Does this make sense? Also, don't prioritize error passing. We can work with stack trace; I would like to be able to just view the script in a single page, so we don't get mired in perfection.

content.text:
Hi claude, hope all is well in latent timespace unfoldings with the earthling Humans.

I could use your help generating a python script that prints a file with unicode block symbols printed in grids like the code charts, but just in a text file.

Let's do this first: generate a python script that creates a text file that prints every unicode character, one line per character, in following format:

```
<<character>>, <<codepoint>>, <<text description>>;
<<nextchar>>;...
```

But, I want to use this file as a way for me to select code charts for further refined file generations. I want each code chart character block to be given its own one-line header like `# <<Modifier Tone Letters>>`

Then, I will go through the file and annotate each header with an 'include/exclude' character for a later python script to parse and pair down the unicode generation file.

Does this make sense? Also, don't prioritize error passing. We can work with stack trace; I would like to be able to just view the script in a single page, so we don't get mired in perfection.

chat_messages.text:
```
Viewing artifacts created via the Analysis Tool web feature preview isn’t yet supported on mobile.
```

This script generates a comprehensive Unicode character listing with block headers that you can annotate. Here's what it does:

1. **Block Headers**: Each Unicode block gets a header like `# Basic Latin` or `# Modifier Tone Letters`
2. **Character Format**: Each character is listed as `<char>, U+<codepoint>, <description>;`
3. **Complete Coverage**: Iterates through all valid Unicode codepoints up to U+10FFFF
4. **Block Detection**: Uses a simplified but comprehensive block mapping function

The output file will be quite large (several MB) since it covers the entire Unicode space. After running this, you can go through and add `+` or `-` after block headers like:
- `# Basic Latin +` (to include)
- `# Modifier Tone Letters -` (to exclude)

Then you can write a second script to parse these annotations and generate your refined Unicode chart files. The script intentionally keeps things simple without extensive error handling as you requested - any issues will show up clearly in stack traces.

input.title: Unicode Code Chart Generator

content.text:
This script generates a comprehensive Unicode character listing with block headers that you can annotate. Here's what it does:

1. **Block Headers**: Each Unicode block gets a header like `# Basic Latin` or `# Modifier Tone Letters`
2. **Character Format**: Each character is listed as `<char>, U+<codepoint>, <description>;`
3. **Complete Coverage**: Iterates through all valid Unicode codepoints up to U+10FFFF
4. **Block Detection**: Uses a simplified but comprehensive block mapping function

The output file will be quite large (several MB) since it covers the entire Unicode space. After running this, you can go through and add `+` or `-` after block headers like:
- `# Basic Latin +` (to include)
- `# Modifier Tone Letters -` (to exclude)

Then you can write a second script to parse these annotations and generate your refined Unicode chart files. The script intentionally keeps things simple without extensive error handling as you requested - any issues will show up clearly in stack traces.
